system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 3: Study 3 - There Will Never Be Another You

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 3 — Study 3 - There Will Never Be Another You

_Pages 13-99._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Diatonic enclosure definition

> A diatonic enclosure is when you play one note above (the 9), then one note below (the 7), and
> finally your target note (the 1).

**Significance (p. 13).** This defines the core melodic device—diatonic enclosure—that Pattern 1 and much of the method's bebop vocabulary is built around.

## Pattern practice method

> Put on a backing track and start improvising, starting each phrase with the pattern you want to
> practice.

**Significance (p. 13).** This is the method's prescribed drill for internalizing any pattern, making it the universal technique-acquisition rule for the entire book.

## Circle of fifths position drill

> A good exercise to get different positions of a pattern under your fingers is by playing it through the
> circle of fifths.

**Significance (p. 13).** This establishes the circle-of-fifths run-through as the standard method for learning all fretboard positions of any pattern.

## Pattern 2 natural 7 in minor

> Note that the pattern uses a natural 7 instead of the b7 that normally occurs in minor scales. The
> natural 7 can be seen as either a chromatic note or the 7 of the melodic minor scale.

**Significance (p. 13).** This rule explains a defining bebop alteration—using the natural 7 over minor chords—and gives the theoretical justification for it.

## Pattern 3 bebop characteristics

> There are two typical bebop characteristics in this lick:
>
> 1. The triplet going from b9 to #9 to b9 with a hammer-on and pull-off.
> 2. The b6 interval starting from the 3rd of the dominant chord.

**Significance (p. 13).** This enumerates the two defining bebop devices embedded in Pattern 3, teaching both the rhythmic technique (hammer-on/pull-off triplet) and the intervallic move (b6 from the 3rd).

## Augmented arpeggio altered sound

> An augmented 7th arpeggio or triad is a convenient way to bring out an altered sound on dominant
> chords.

**Significance (p. 13).** This is a concise rule linking the augmented arpeggio to altered dominant harmony, which is foundational to jazz improvisation vocabulary.

## Half-diminished sub over dominant

> Playing a half-diminished arpeggio from the 3rd of a dominant chord brings out the 9 sound.
> For example: Bm7b5 over G7.

**Significance (p. 13).** This defines the Bm7b5-over-G7 substitution rule with its harmonic rationale, a reusable device that recurs in Patterns 5, 19, and 55.

## Harmonic minor over V7alt

> On a minor ii V I, you can play the tonic harmonic minor scale over the V7alt chord.
> For example: over Bm7b5-E7alt-Am7 (ii VI in A minor), you play A harmonic minor over the
>
> E7alt chord.
>
> When doing so, you create a 7b9,b13 sound over that chord.
>
> Another name for the harmonic minor scale played over a dominant chord is the Phrygian dominant
> scale.
>
> | A harmonic minor = E Phrygian dominant

**Significance (p. 13).** This passage defines the harmonic minor / Phrygian dominant equivalence and the specific alteration tones it produces, giving players a concrete scale-choice rule for minor ii-V-I contexts.

## Doubling notes technique

> Pattern 18 uses a simple, but effective technique that I learned from transcribing Pat Metheny solos:
>
> doubling notes.
>
> It can be applied to every scale, arpeggio, or pattern you can think of.

**Significance (p. 13).** This introduces the doubling-notes technique as a universally applicable melodic tool, not just a one-off pattern, expanding its relevance across the entire method.

## Fmaj7 sub over half-diminished

> You can play a maj7 chord over a half-diminished chord, starting on the b5 of that half-
> diminished chord. For example: Fmaj7 over Bm7b5

**Significance (p. 13).** This states the generalized substitution rule for coloring half-diminished chords with a major-7 arpeggio, producing the beautiful #11 tension.

## Ab minor triad sus4/altered sound

> The triad brings out a sus4 sound (C) with the altered tensions b9 (Ab) and b13 (Eb).

**Significance (p. 13).** This explains exactly which chord tones and tensions the Ab minor triad generates over G7, giving players a harmonic rationale for the substitution.

## Pattern 26 hammer/pull technique

> Pattern 26 is a typical Pat Metheny phrase, where the 16th notes are played with a hammer on and
> two pull offs.

**Significance (p. 13).** This specifies the precise right-hand/left-hand technique required to execute Pattern 26's characteristic 16th-note figure.

## Dm over G7 (Wes technique)

> Playing Dm over G7 is a technique Wes Montgomery would often use in his solos.

**Significance (p. 13).** This names a specific stylistic substitution—Dm triad over G7—attributing it to Wes Montgomery and validating its use in Pattern 25's dominant context.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
